script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Friday — here is your QQQ recap. The portfolio closed one point four zero percent down on the session.

Nvidia narrowly held its title as the world's most valuable company by market capitalization — but only barely. Apple crossed above Nvidia in early trading, and at the close, the margin between the two was razor thin.

Chip stocks led a broad technology selloff on Friday, dragging the Nasdaq sharply lower. Geopolitical tensions and renewed skepticism around artificial intelligence valuations were cited as the primary drivers. No major macro data was scheduled, leaving sentiment and positioning to do most of the work on a volatile session.

The dominant story of Friday's session was the deepening semiconductor rout, and Nvidia sat at the center of it. The stock fell two point two one percent, and at Friday's close, Nvidia's market capitalization stood at four point nine one trillion dollars — just barely ahead of Apple's four point nine trillion, according to Dow Jones Market Data. That near-crossing was not incidental. It reflects a genuine shift in investor sentiment around artificial intelligence infrastructure spending. Analysts at Yahoo Finance flagged valuation concerns directly, noting that while Nvidia carries a fifty-five point six percent net margin and generated ninety-six point seven billion dollars in free cash flow, the stock's premium is increasingly under pressure as broader AI enthusiasm moderates. For this portfolio, where Nvidia is the single largest holding at seven point six percent, that margin compression in sentiment is a meaningful headwind.

Nvidia was down two point two one percent on the session. As noted, the near-loss of its title as the world's most valuable company underscores just how much the AI trade is being repriced right now. The key watch is next week's earnings calendar and whether any major hyperscaler reaffirms capital expenditure guidance.

Apple was up zero point one four percent on the session — the only primary holding to close in positive territory on Friday. Apple briefly surpassed Nvidia in market capitalization during intraday trading, a symbolic moment that Fox Business and Dow Jones both confirmed. That resilience, even marginal, stands out on a day this red. Watch Alphabet's earnings next week for read-through on the broader technology advertising and infrastructure narrative.

Turning to Alphabet, the stock fell two point one seven percent on the session. Earnings are directly ahead, and analyst commentary flagged that capital expenditure guidance will be critical for the battered artificial intelligence trade. Dow Jones Futures coverage specifically named Alphabet as a linchpin for what comes next in AI sentiment. At six point one eight percent of the portfolio, this one carries weight going into the report.

Micron Technology fell zero point five zero percent on the session. Seeking Alpha noted Friday that the semiconductor exchange-traded fund known as SOXX may be turning structurally bearish, despite solid recent earnings from Taiwan Semiconductor and ASML. Separately, reporting confirmed Micron closed below the one trillion dollar market capitalization threshold on Thursday for the first time since early June. That is a level worth watching.

Microsoft was down one point eight two percent on the session. The stock moved in line with the broader technology selloff, with no company-specific catalyst reported. Valuation pressure across large-cap software and cloud names was the dominant theme.

Over at Amazon, shares fell one point zero six percent. The company was referenced in the context of Meta's reported talks with Anthropic about leasing computing infrastructure — a deal that would put Meta in direct competition with Amazon in cloud services. That competitive dynamic is worth monitoring.

Advanced Micro Devices fell one point zero three percent on the session. No company-specific news drove the move — this was broad semiconductor weakness pulling the stock lower alongside its peers. Upcoming earnings from peer companies will provide the next meaningful signal.

Tesla dropped two point six one percent on Friday — among the steeper declines across the primary holdings. Dow Jones Futures coverage flagged Tesla as a key name in the week ahead, with earnings on the calendar. That report will be the next major event for this position.

Intel fell two point zero zero percent on the session. The broad chip selloff was the primary driver, consistent with the Applied Materials decline of five point five seven percent and Lam Research's drop of two point three nine percent seen elsewhere in the semiconductor complex. Intel's own earnings report is also approaching and will be watched closely.

Three data points to track over the next forty-eight hours. First, Alphabet reports earnings — capital expenditure guidance will set the tone for the entire artificial intelligence infrastructure trade and carries direct implications for multiple holdings here. Second, Tesla reports earnings — execution on margins and delivery volume will be the focus. Third, monitor any weekend geopolitical developments tied to the Middle East, given that rebounding oil prices were cited Friday as a compounding factor in the selloff.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Alphabet's earnings report is the single most consequential event on the near-term horizon — what management says about spending and demand will likely set the direction for this entire portfolio early next week.
